title: Psalms 41
subtitle: Book I Doxology: Blessed Is He Who Has Regard for the Weak

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — “Blessed Is the One Who Considers the Poor”

heb.paragraph: "Blessed is the one who has regard for the weak (dal)" (v.1). The psalm opens with a beatitude for compassion. Those who care for the vulnerable will themselves be protected (vv.1–3). David then describes his own illness: enemies wish him dead, a close friend has betrayed him. "Even my close friend, whom I trusted, he who shared my bread, has turned against me" (v.9) — Jesus applies this to Judas (John 13:18).

notes.note: "Blessed is the one who has regard for the weak." The psalm opens Ps 1-style: a beatitude. But here the blessing is for compassion, not Torah-meditation. God protects those who protect others.

"Even my close friend, whom I trusted, he who shared my bread, has lifted up his heel against me." Jesus quotes this at the Last Supper (John 13:18), identifying Judas as the betrayer. The shared-bread-turned-betrayal is the psalm’s most christological moment.

refs.note: The beatitude blessing the merciful parallels the psalm's opening blessing on those who regard the weak.
James's teaching that mercy triumphs over judgment echoes the psalm's promise that the merciful receive mercy.
The proverb that lending to the poor is lending to the LORD reflects the same theology of reciprocal compassion.

hist.context: Psalm 41 closes Book I of the Psalter (Pss 1-41) with a doxology. The superscription attributes it to David. The psalm combines wisdom teaching (blessed are the compassionate) with individual lament (betrayal by a close friend). The reference to being raised from a sickbed suggests a context of illness compounded by social abandonment.

sections.header: Verses 10–13 — “Praise Be to the LORD, the God of Israel, from Everlasting to Everlasting”

heb.paragraph: "Praise be (bārūk̅) to the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen" (v.13). The doxology that closes Book I of the Psalter. Each of the five books ends with a doxology (41:13; 72:18–19; 89:52; 106:48; 150). The double "Amen" is emphatic: so be it, so be it. Book I (Psalms 1–41) is complete.

notes.note: "Raise me up, that I may repay them." David asks for restoration. "I know that you are pleased with me, for my enemy does not triumph over me." The confidence: God’s pleasure, not just God’s power.
"Praise be to the LORD, from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen." Book I’s closing doxology. The Psalter’s first 41 psalms end in eternal praise.

refs.note: Jesus quotes verse 9 ('he who shared my bread has lifted his heel against me') as fulfilled in Judas's betrayal.
Peter cites this psalm among scriptures fulfilled in Judas, linking David's betrayer to Christ's.
The one who dips bread with Jesus at the Last Supper becomes the betrayer, echoing the shared-bread imagery.

hist.context: The betrayal by a 'close friend' (literally 'man of my peace') who ate bread with David has been connected to Ahithophel's defection to Absalom (2 Sam 15-17). The concluding doxology ('Praise be to the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting') is a standard formula marking the end of Psalter collections.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Book I Doxology: Blessed Is He Who Has Regard for the Weak.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God protects those who protect others…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI said, “Have mercy on me, Lord; heal me, for I have sinned against you.”ESVAs for me, I said, \u201cO LORD, be gracious to me; heal me, for I have sinned against you!\u201dNIV“A vile disease has afflicted him; he will never get up from the place where he lies.”ESVThey say, \u201cA deadly thing is poured out on him; he will not rise again from where he lies.\u201d

rec.text: ""Blessed is the one who has regard for the weak." The psalm opens Ps 1-style: a beatitude. But here the blessing is for compassion, not Torah-meditation. God protects those who protect others."

rows.label: Book I Closes — 41:10–13
rows.text: "Praise be from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen." The first doxology.

hebtext.note: "Blessed is the one who has regard for the weak (dal)" (v.1). The psalm opens with a beatitude for compassion. Those who care for the vulnerable will themselves be protected (vv.1–3). David then describes his own illness: enemies wish him dead, a close friend has betrayed him. "Even my close friend, whom I trusted, he who shared my bread, has turned against me" (v.9) — Jesus applies this to Judas (John 13:18).

"Praise be (bārūk̅) to the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen" (v.13). The doxology that closes Book I of the Psalter. Each of the five books ends with a doxology (41:13; 72:18–19; 89:52; 106:48; 150). The double "Amen" is emphatic: so be it, so be it. Book I (Psalms 1–41) is complete.

themes.note: Prophecy: Judas-betrayal (John 13:18). Worship: Book I’s closing doxology. Mercy: blessing for those who care for the weak.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Blessed is the one who has regard for the weak,' consider asking God whether your concern for vulnerable people is active or theoretical — and whether the weak in your community would say they have experienced your regard.
